nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving tributaries, shaded creeks, and forest streams with soft sandy or silty substrate, leaf litter, and overhanging riparian vegetation; typically blackwater or clearwater conditions

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate to protect delicate barbels
driftwood and twisted roots for shelter
dark substrate to enhance natural coloration

diet.notes: Foraging omnivore; accepts high-quality sinking pellets, catfish wafers, granules, and flake foods. Benefits from regular live and frozen offerings — bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia, Tubifex, and mosquito larvae. Also takes blanched vegetables such as cucumber and zucchini. Should not rely solely on leftovers from other tank inhabitants; target feeding with sinking foods ensures adequate nutrition.

avoidWith: boisterous or fast-moving fish that outcompete for food

summary: A petite, endearing Peruvian Amazon catfish reaching ~5 cm, named for its distinctive dark facial mask. Peaceful, sociable, and easy to keep in soft-acidic water; best in groups of 6+. Note: reclassified as Hoplisoma atropersonatum by Dias & Britto (2024) — widely sold under the Corydoras name.
careNotes: The Fairy Cory is a small, peaceful bottom-dweller from the Tigre and Nanay drainage systems of Ecuador and Peru, inhabiting soft, slightly acidic, slow-moving forest streams. It reaches approximately 4.5–5 cm and is among the more delicate-looking Corydoras, adorned with a dark mask across the eyes and fine mosaic patterning. A group of at least 6 individuals is strongly recommended — solitary or pairs show stress and reduced activity. Soft, fine sand substrate is essential; coarse gravel abrades their sensitive barbels, which can lead to bacterial infection and rapid deterioration. A minimum 60 L tank with good filtration and regular water changes maintains the soft, slightly acidic conditions preferred. Like all Corydoras, the Fairy Cory is a facultative air-breather and will periodically dart to the surface to gulp air — a small gap between water surface and cover glass is important. Low-moderate lighting with ample cover of driftwood, leaf litter, and plants provides security. Avoid hard alkaline water; this species does best below pH 7.0 and in very soft water (gH 1–12). Compatible with small, peaceful community species of similar size. Note: a 2024 phylogenomic revision by Dias et al. published in the Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society resurrected the genus Hoplisoma, placing this species as Hoplisoma atropersonatum — this reclassification is pending broader trade and database adoption. Warning: like all Corydoras/Hoplisoma, the pectoral and dorsal fin spines can inflict a painful wound and are venomous — handle with care when netting.
breedingNotes: Moderately easy to breed in soft, slightly acidic water. Use a ratio of 2 males per female. Condition breeders with frequent live and frozen foods. Trigger spawning with a large (50–70%) water change using cooler, soft water and increased oxygenation; repeat daily until spawning occurs. During the T-position embrace, females hold 2–4 eggs between their pelvic fins at a time, scattering up to 75 adhesive eggs total across plant leaves and tank surfaces. Remove adults after spawning. Incubation is approximately 3–4 days at 22–24°C. Once fry have absorbed their yolk sacs, offer microworm, Artemia nauplii, and fine powdered fry foods.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras atropersonatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (45–50 mm SL), temperature (21–27°C), pH (5.0–7.0), hardness (36–179 ppm ≈ gH 2–10), native range (Tigre and Nanay drainages, Ecuador/Peru), habitat, diet (foraging omnivore), breeding protocol (2:1 male/female ratio, cool-water trigger, 3–4 day incubation), and behavioral notes (peaceful, gregarious, groups of 4–6+).

additional.name: FishBase — Corydoras atropersonatus (Hoplisoma atropersonatum)
additional.notes: Confirmed max size 4.5 cm SL, temperature 21–24°C, pH 6.0–8.0, hardness dH 2–25, family Callichthyidae, order Siluriformes, South America / Upper Amazon basin distribution, demersal freshwater. Notes genus as Hoplisoma atropersonatum following the 2024 Dias et al. revision.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Corydoradinae revision changes names for hundreds of species
additional.notes: Covered the 2024 Dias et al. phylogenomic revision (Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society) resurrecting Hoplisoma for former Corydoras lineage L6/L9 species, placing atropersonatus as Hoplisoma atropersonatum. Confirms broad trade adoption is pending.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk — Fairy Cory (Hoplisoma atropersonatum)
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (4.5 cm), temperature (22–26°C), pH (5.5–7.5), hardness (up to 18 dH), substrate requirements (sandy), group size (5+), diet (pellets, granules, flake, frozen foods), and breeding T-position details (up to 75 eggs scattered). Lists species under new name Hoplisoma atropersonatum.

additional.name: Aquarium Fish Source — Corydoras atropersonatus Fairy Spotted Cory Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (~4.5 cm), temperature (21–24°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (2–25 dGH), tank minimum (75 L / 20 gal), diet (omnivore — pellets, frozen foods, vegetables), native range (Ecuador and Peru, Upper Amazon). Corroborates barbel sensitivity to coarse substrate.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer; T-position spawning triggered by cool-water changes. Ratio of 2 males per female recommended. Up to 75 adhesive eggs scattered across plants and surfaces; incubation 3–4 days. Remove adults post-spawning. Fry accept microworm and Artemia nauplii once yolk sac absorbed.
